name: BSP Tree (Binary Space Partitioning)
def: A binary tree that recursively splits space by arbitrary (often non-axis-aligned) hyperplanes, ordering geometry for visibility and spatial queries.
problem: Partition and order polygons in a scene — classically exact back-to-front visibility without a depth buffer.
mechanism: Each node holds a splitting plane; geometry is partitioned into front/back half-spaces (splitting any polygon that straddles the plane), recursively, until leaves hold convex regions. Traversing relative to the viewpoint yields a strict back-to-front (or front-to-back) order.
benefits: Exact, view-independent visibility ordering from a precomputed tree
Fast point-location and inside/outside tests; underpins CSG
limits: Expensive to build; splitting straddling polygons inflates their count
Static — rebuilding for dynamic geometry is costly
Plane-selection heuristic dominates quality (balance vs splits)
failures: Poor split choices bloating the tree with fragments
Numerical robustness when classifying coplanar geometry
whenNot: Highly dynamic scenes (use a BVH or spatial hash)
Axis-aligned point queries where a k-d tree or grid is simpler
myths: "BSP trees died with Doom/Quake" — they live on in collision, CSG and visibility (PVS) preprocessing
